evidence_quotes.quote: In the Video Games industry, End User License Agreement (EULA), Terms of Service (ToS), Rules of Conduct (RoC) and similar contractual agreements are unreadable to the average consumer and as a result most will simply scroll down to the accept-button without knowing what they have agreed to.

these types of contracts are unfair by nature because no consumer have been part of the process of formulating the contract conditions (no negotiation), the corporation that has drafted the contract have a written clause that they may change its terms at any time, and they are self-signing.

Ever since the emergence of the internet big corporations have found ever increasingly greedy methods to collect data on consumers to the point that they can easily know what you do on a daily basis, what you had for breakfast, when you wake up and go to bed, and everything in between.

paragraphs.text: Dear EU-representatives, in recent years you have done some great things to modernize regulations and amendments to existing laws, such as GDPR, Accessibility (EN 301 549), CPC action against predatory MTX in video games, DSA, and more. However, I feel that it is not enough in an age of digitization. Ever since the emergence of the internet big corporations have found ever increasingly greedy methods to collect data on consumers to the point that they can easily know what you do on a daily basis, what you had for breakfast, when you wake up and go to bed, and everything in between. And what do they use all this data for? To constantly push advertisements down your throat. As if this wasnt bad enough that they (red. The big corporations) want to control every aspect of your life as a consumer, they also want to control what you spend your hard-earned money on by engaging in Anti-Rights to Repair, and Anti-Rights to Own, generally Anti-Consumer Rights practices. One area where I see particularly egregious bad behavior from big corporations is in the Video Games industry. End User License Agreement (EULA), Terms of Service (ToS), Rules of Conduct (RoC) and similar contractual agreements are unreadable to the average consumer and as a result most will simply scroll down to the accept-button without knowing what they have agreed to. The other thing is that these types of contracts are unfair by nature because no consumer have been part of the process of formulating the contract conditions (no negotiation), the corporation that has drafted the contract have a written clause that they may change its terms at any time, and they are self-signing. Current Contract laws/regulations need to be updated to the digital age that were in, there needs to be provisions within the regulations that ensure fairness when it comes to self-signing-contracts. In the Video Games industry, it could be something in the line of When a (digital) product has been, or could reasonably have been understood to be, sold to a consumer said consumer now owns that copy of the product, and has the right to do with it what he wants (within reason). Another unfair practice that exists within Video Games industry is the practice of planned obsolescence which didnt exist until the internet was readily available to the average consumer, at reasonable speeds, this practice only serves two (2) functions: 1) Ending official support of a game when its commercial viability is no longer present. 2) Forcing a game community to purchase the next installment in the series IP and rendering the old unplayable. I wholeheartedly is in support of the Digital Fairness Act (DFA) as a step in the right direction for better Consumer Rights.
